chunk_summary: Guidelines for empiric antibiotic regimens in severe acute malnutrition (SAM), including dosages, step-down criteria, and renal safety protocols for both outpatient and inpatient care.
learning_objectives: Understand the empiric antibiotic regimens for managing infections in SAM, including dosages and administration routes.
Identify step-down criteria for transitioning from IV/IM to oral antibiotics in SAM management.
Recognize renal safety considerations, including withholding gentamicin in cases of oliguria or anuria.
Learn escalation protocols for worsening clinical status or septic shock in SAM patients.
key_facts: Outpatient SAM: Oral amoxicillin 15 mg/kg per dose every 8 hours for 5 days, unless local policy suggests otherwise.
Inpatient SAM: Ampicillin 50 mg/kg per dose IV/IM every 6 hours plus gentamicin 7.5 mg/kg once daily for 7 days.
Add cloxacillin 100 mg/kg/day in 4 divided doses for suspected staphylococcal infections (e.g., skin lesions or abscess).
Escalate to cefotaxime 150 mg/kg/day (3 doses) or ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg/day (2 doses) if no improvement in 48–72 hours.
Withhold gentamicin if oliguria/anuria is present; reassess hydration, perfusion, and renal function before restarting.
Step down to oral therapy when the child is clinically improving, tolerating feeds, and can complete the course reliably.
reference_quote: Empiric antibiotics in SAM must be 'ready to prescribe,' because invasive infection can present without fever and delays increase mortality. (Source: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, 2011)
document_key_topics: Renal safety protocols in SAM management
